hub_subtitle: Inglês para Trabalho Remoto em Tecnologia & Quality Assurance

lesson.menu_title: Reported Speech -- Capturing What Was Said in Meetings and Escalations
lesson.menu_desc: After a night incident, Ana debriefs her manager on an escalation call: she reports what the client, QA and the on-call engineer said, shifting the tenses back and keeping the exact wording where it matters -- David said they had deployed a bad config, and the client asked whether we could send a summary that day -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 17 -- Reported Speech
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Claudia Veraldi | Lesson 17 -- Reported Speech

listenings.text: Let's talk about how to report what people said in a meeting, accurately, without twisting it. When you move from someone's exact words to a report, one thing usually happens: the tense shifts back. If your colleague said 'the build is ready', you report that she said the build was ready. 'Is' becomes 'was'. 'We deployed it today' becomes 'they had deployed it that day'. 'I will fix it' becomes 'he said he would fix it'. Present moves to past, will moves to would, can moves to could. That backshift is not decoration; it tells the listener you are quoting the past, not the present. Now, two warnings. First, do not editorialize. Your job is to relay what was said, not to add your own opinion. If you slip your judgment into the report, people stop trusting you. Second, when the exact wording matters, a number, a commitment, a deadline, do not paraphrase it. Quote it verbatim. Say 'her words were, quote, we ship on Friday, end quote'. For everything else, a clean paraphrase is fine, as long as the meaning survives. Report faithfully, shift the tense, and keep the wording honest. That is how people learn to trust your summaries.

Hi Ana, it's Nadia from the account side, just a quick voice note before you write up the escalation. I was on the call with the client, and I want to make sure the right words go up the chain. The client said the outage had cost them a full morning of orders, and they asked whether we could guarantee it would not happen again. Please notify the incident manager about that, because it needs to go up the chain of command, not stay in our little thread. One thing: when you report the client's commitment, keep it verbatim. Their exact words were, we will accept the fix if you confirm it in writing by Friday. Do not paraphrase that part, because the recipient will act on it. Everything else you can summarize in your own words, just keep the meaning faithful. And please acknowledge my note so I know it reached you. Thanks, Ana, you are really good at this.

extra_audio.text: Here is how Ana reported the escalation call to her manager. First, she listened carefully and noted the exact wording of what each person had said. Then, she shifted the tenses back so the whole report was in reported speech. After that, she paraphrased the long explanations but kept the key numbers and commitments verbatim. Next, she checked for any discrepancy between what people said and what the logs showed. Finally, she relayed the clean summary to the recipients on the incident thread.
